title: agent-runtime: scheduling gaps - cron, event triggers, rate limiting
summary: Issue #592 tracks daemon trigger hardening for packages/agent-runtime and the duplicated packages/agent-platform daemon implementation. The current daemon supports only file/webhook/timer triggers, parses a small five-field local-time cron subset, queues triggers in memory behind maxConcurrentRuns, and lacks trigger admission control, deduplication, retry metadata, dead letter handling, and failure metrics.

relatedIssues.reason: Crash recovery and persistent queue work should own durable replay primitives that retry/DLQ behavior can integrate with.
Background process lifecycle and backpressure overlap with scheduled background task execution, but issue #592 is specifically trigger admission and scheduling.

triageFindings: packages/agent-runtime/src/daemon/config.ts validates only file, webhook, and timer trigger types.
packages/agent-runtime/src/daemon/timerScheduler.ts parses five cron fields and uses local Date getters.
packages/agent-runtime/src/daemon/fileWatcher.ts debounces per watcher but does not deduplicate overlapping rules.

packages/agent-runtime/src/daemon/webhookListener.ts accepts valid webhook bodies and immediately calls onTrigger with a 200 response; it has request body limits and bearer auth but no admission policy.
packages/agent-runtime/src/daemon/loop.ts stores pending triggers in an in-memory TriggerEvent[] and gates only by maxConcurrentRuns.
packages/agent-platform/src/daemon/* duplicates the daemon implementation and currently has richer daemon test coverage than agent-runtime.
docs/agent-layer-gaps.md records the same gaps under no event-driven triggers, no trigger rate limiting, no trigger deduplication, no dead letter queue, and in-memory-only queue limitations.

acceptanceFocus: Cron handling preserves current five-field numeric, comma, range, and step behavior while adding explicitly tested support for named months/days, timezone-aware matching, supported macros such as @daily, and well-defined unsupported behavior for L/#/@reboot if deferred.
Trigger config and runtime support an extensible event-source abstraction for queue, git, and chat-style sources without forcing unavailable external services or credentials for local daemon startup.
Webhook and event-source admission control exposes bounded queue depth, max-per-window rate limiting, defer/reject outcomes, backpressure-aware responses, and queue/rate-limit metrics or log events.
Deduplication uses stable fingerprints and logs every suppressed duplicate while avoiding false suppression of legitimate distinct rules or payloads.

Failed triggers do not crash the daemon; retry attempts, backoff metadata, and DLQ-style failed-trigger records are persisted or recorded in a way that can later integrate with #585 durable queue work.
packages/agent-runtime and packages/agent-platform daemon behavior are centralized or protected by parity tests so the duplicate implementations cannot drift silently.
Existing file/webhook/timer configurations remain backward compatible unless the capability spec identifies an explicit migration path.
docs/agent-layer-gaps.md and runtime README text reflect only the capabilities actually implemented and leave coordinated #585/#593 work as follow-ups.
nonGoals: Do not implement full crash recovery or restart replay from #585.
Do not implement full background process lifecycle management from #593.
Do not require RabbitMQ, Kafka, SQS, Slack, Discord, or git provider credentials for unit tests or daemon startup.
Do not introduce a competing durable queue design if #585 owns the shared persistence primitive.
Do not silently accept unsupported cron syntax with ambiguous behavior.
Do not generate shell-oriented process subtasks for this repository workflow.
qualityGates: Phase 0 reuse audit is rendered before new trigger infrastructure is added.
A staged capability spec defines accepted scope and non-goals before implementation.
Regression tests are written before implementation for cron semantics, event-source abstraction, admission control, deduplication, retry/DLQ handling, and daemon parity.
Targeted daemon tests pass for both agent-runtime and agent-platform or for a shared centralized daemon module with package-level parity coverage.
Full agent-runtime and agent-platform test suites pass, or unrelated pre-existing failures are documented with exact evidence.
Builds pass for affected runtime/platform packages.
Security review confirms trigger payloads, logs, and DLQ records avoid leaking secrets and request limits/backpressure resist trivial denial-of-service.
Compatibility review confirms existing daemon config and automation rules keep working.

plannedPhases.name: Issue context
plannedPhases.purpose: Read #592, comments, labels, and related issue references to lock scope and boundaries.

plannedPhases.name: Reuse audit
plannedPhases.purpose: Render repo-required reuse-audit findings before adding trigger infrastructure.

plannedPhases.name: Trigger runtime trace
plannedPhases.purpose: Map daemon trigger call paths, duplicated platform code, reusable automation types, and existing test coverage.

plannedPhases.name: Capability spec
plannedPhases.purpose: Define staged cron, event-source, admission, deduplication, retry/DLQ, and parity semantics before code changes.

plannedPhases.name: Regression coverage
plannedPhases.purpose: Add failing acceptance and unit tests for every accepted trigger hardening slice.

plannedPhases.name: Incremental implementation
plannedPhases.purpose: Implement the accepted slices in small test-backed changes, preserving existing trigger behavior.

plannedPhases.name: Verification loop
plannedPhases.purpose: Run targeted and full package gates, iterating up to three attempts when tests or review find regressions.

plannedPhases.name: Security and compatibility review
plannedPhases.purpose: Review denial-of-service surfaces, secret handling, cron compatibility, false deduplication, and runtime/platform parity.

plannedPhases.name: Final acceptance
plannedPhases.purpose: Confirm issue acceptance, documented deferred work, clean scoped diff, and delivery-ready PR/issue summaries.
